The issue with the function above is that it does a lot of stuff. It branches on whether the figure is a class or an object. If it's a class, we don't create marks. If it's an object, we pull scales from the existing figure and create marks using those scales.

Long `if`/`else` statements suggest that we can split up this method into two pieces. In this case, it looks like we can split this code into a `_create_marks` method and a `_create_fig` method.

Then we can do something like

```python
def scatter(...):
    ...
    fig = fig or _create_fig(...)
    [scat] = _create_marks(fig=fig, ...)
    fig._add_marks([scat])
    ...
```

which should make the individual methods a lot easier to write.

This is incorrect. In order to release locks held from other places you need to use `reset()`. The force option is there for sloppy code that don't always acquire it (or didn't check if it was acquired properly).

Example code:

```python

lock = Lock(conn, "foo")
try:
  if lock.acquire(block=False):
    print("Got the lock. Do crazy dance")
  else:
    print("Didn't get the lock. Do normal dance")
finally:
  lock.release(force=True)
```

Is this basically "match strings that end in 3 or more uppercase characters followed by 's'"? If so, I think this expression is more clear and possibly more efficient: `[A-Z]{2,}s$`

```
>>> re.compile('[A-Z]{2,}s$', re.DEBUG)
max_repeat 2 4294967295
  in
    range (65, 90)
literal 115
at at_end
```

Compared to

```
>>> re.compile('[A-Z][A-Z][A-Z]+s$', re.DEBUG)
in
  range (65, 90)
in
  range (65, 90)
max_repeat 1 4294967295
  in
    range (65, 90)
literal 115
at at_end
```

I haven't benchmarked this to see if it makes a difference, but I think using `[A-Z]{2,}s$` is easier to read.

This `'use strict'` is still needed, since this file (and the others under `neutrino-custom/*` is run by node.js, rather than by the browser - and node doesn't yet support modules natively (other than when started with `--experimental-modules`), and even then the file would need to have file extension `.mjs` to be treated as a module (which is currently a point of contention within the node / JS community). More info here:
https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html

As such in my local Neutrino branch I do something like:
```js
// `use strict` is still necessary here since this file is not treated as a module.
'use strict'; // eslint-disable-line strict, lines-around-directive

// ...
```

The main bug is in the condition `if self.padding > 0:` and it should be fixed.

Next, you propose an update of padding argument acceptable value as actually, according to the doc of [`RandomCrop`](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/transforms/transforms.py#L367) it says:
```
padding (int or sequence, optional): Optional padding on each border
            of the image. Default is 0, i.e no padding. If a sequence of length
            4 is provided, it is used to pad left, top, right, bottom borders
            respectively.
```
so, we logically should not provide a tuple like `(16, 4)`. But as you are right in functional there is an option to set padding as [2-element tuple](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/transforms/functional.py#L220).
But as it is said in the doc on padding:
> If tuple of length 2 is provided this is the padding on left/right and top/bottom respectively

So it is correct to get in your example `RandomCrop(size=(128, 32), padding=(16, 4))` the result you describe.

Maybe we need to uniformize all these arguments and fix the condition by setting `padding=None`  instead of `padding=0` as no padding...

So the actual use case is that we want to use process scanning in GRR, but I feel like this complements the process scanning API you already have nicely in the general case.

The problem I'm trying to solve is that when scan all processes for a signature and it tells me, for example, that the internet explorer process is a match, I do not have any good way to use this information. I'd like to confirm that there is something shady going on with that process (or discard as a FP) but I can't just look at the file - it's the IE and therefore benign - I need to get memory in order to do that. Of course I could use a different library to get said memory but using the same mechanism that also yielded the initial signature match seems just more robust to me.

If you are worried about the functionality being in yara-python we could still go the other route we discussed and have a dedicated process memory acquisition library, potentially with its own Python wrapper lib. Either option would work for me as long as both the scanning and the process dumping mechanism use the same backend.

> I see, thanks. So it is not clear-specific but just a general things about variables in slots. My approach, once again, would be to fix those cases one-by-one with, as you suggested, bumping persistent version for the slots ghostification patch.

Yes, it is `clear` specific. `clear` is (now) being called when the object's state is undefined. The state that's was supposed to be an invariant, established by `__new__` and maintained by `__setstate__`. That invariant is now broken, and calling a method when the invariants of the class do not hold is a sure way to have trouble (not to mention just being rude). Since the invariant is now broken, `__setstate__` should not call into code that *may or may not* rely on that invariant---it has no way of knowing.

It is far better to avoid introducing trouble at the chokepoint, where it can be fixed all at once---the superclass--than expect everyone else to go clean up when their code breaks.

> It would be a pity not to be able to rely on ghostification semantics to completely release objects.

First, we've never been able to rely on that. persistent has never done that. This change obviously breaks a number of cases. We've located two just by looking at a handful of (very widely used) projects. 

It starting to seem to me that changing this and breaking unknown downstream projects is far more of a risk than simply using `__dict__` for objects where allowing `__slots__` to remain is a problem. 

Second, it's *still* not a guarantee. When the object is implemented in C, if it doesn't specifically do something with its struct members, they aren't released. This is the only reason that zope.container's Proxy still works in the C implementation after this change---the struct member that is `_wrapped` is left sitting untouched by `_p_deactivate`.
